coredata.dc:title: Signal-on electrochemiluminescent immunosensor based on poly(amidoamine) dendrimer functionalized carbon nanodots amplification for ultrasensitive detection of α-fetoprotein
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shupei
Zang, Lele
Zhang, Xiaozhen
Dai, Hong
Xu, Guifang
Zhang, Qingrong
Yang, Caiping
Lin, Yanyu
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive sandwich-type electrochemiluminescent biosensor based on carbonaceous based nanomaterials was fabricated for alpha-fetoprotein detection. Herein, the composite of fullerene, graphene oxide and chitosan with specific surface area, good water dispersibility, and excellent biocompatibility was first modified onto the electrode surface and then it was in situ electrochemical reduced to improve the conductivity. After the capturing antibody was immobilized onto the modified electrode surface, the detection antibody together with poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots as the recognizable signal tags was self-assembled onto the electrode by typical sandwich immune reaction. By this design, the abundant functional groups, good water dispersibility and good electroconductivity of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots make the composite not only an immobilizing platform to carrier numerous detection antibodies, but also an promising promoter for the electrochemiluminescent signal of luminol. With the aforementioned amplify factor, the established electrochemiluminescent biosensor under the optimal conditions demonstrated a wider dynamic response from 1fgmL−1 to 80ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.33fgmL−1. Additionally, the experimental results exhibited the immunosensor possessed excellent stability, reproducibility and selectively. Accordingly, this immunosensing strategy for alpha-fetoprotein with the assistance of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots provided a promising approach in clinical disease scanning.
coredata.dcterms:subject: electrochemiluminescent
biosensor
alpha-fetoprotein
carbon nanodots
poly(amidoamine) dendrimers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GO, C60, CNDs, GO@C60, PAADs@CNDs and PAADs@CNDs based signal tags (PAADs@CNDs@Ab2)
Characteristics of the prepared GO and C60
Electrochemical behavior of various modified electrodes
The tentative mechanism for the AFP detection
ECL immunosensor characterization and detection strategy feasibility assay
Optimal conditions for the ECL biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammetries (A, B), Chronocoulometries (C) and Current-v (D) of different modified electrodes in 0.1molL−1 KCl solution in the presence (A, C, D) and absence (B) of 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−. (a) GO, (b) GCE, (c) rGO, (d) rGO@C60. The inset in C is the linear relationship between the charge and the square roots of the time.

(A) ECL responses of different modified electrodes in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 8.0) contained 20μmolL−1 luminol. (a) GCE, (b) rGO@C60, (c) rGO@C60/Ab1, (d) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag, (e) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/PAADs@CNDs@Ab2. (B) ECL response of different modified electrodes in 0.1molL−1 pH8.0 PBS solution contained 2×10−5 molL−1 luminol, (a) GCE, (b) rGO, (c) rGO@C60, (d) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/Ab2, (e) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/PAADs@Ab2, (f) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/CNDs@Ab2, (g) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/PAADs@CNDs@Ab2. ECL responses of (C) different electrodes and (D) rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag towards different signal probes in 0.1M PBS (pH 8.0) solution contained 2×10−5 molL−1 luminol.

The currents on rGO@C60 modified electrode of different GO concentration (A) and C60 concentration (B). Concentration: (A) 0.3% CS, 1mgmL−1C60 and (B) 0.3% CS, 2mgmL−1 GO. Effect of different incubation time of Ab1 (C) and Ag (D) on ECL response.
Effect of (A) the volume ration of PAADs and CNDs, (B) volume of Ab2, and (C) incubation time of PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 on the ECL intensities.

(A) ECL-E curves of the sensor towards different concentration of AFP. (a–i) 10−6, 10−5, 10−4, 10−2, 1, 10, 20, 40, 80ngmL−1. (B) The corresponding calibration curve. (C) The ECL signal responses of the sensor in the presence of various interfering antigens and their mixture. 80ngmL−1 AFP, and 160ngmL−1 CEA, BSA, hIgG, HSA, hTG, PSA. (D) The stability of the ECL sensor.
Schematic illustration of the electrochemiluminescent immunosensor developed process. The inset were the TEM image of GO and C60.

head.ce:title: Signal-on electrochemiluminescent immunosensor based on poly(amidoamine) dendrimer functionalized carbon nanodots amplification for ultrasensitive detection of α-fetoprotein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350108,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gynaecological Oncology, Fujian provincial Cancer Hospital, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nuclear Medicine Department, The Affiliated People’s Hospital of Fujian University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated People’s Hospital of Fujian University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive sandwich-type electrochemiluminescent biosensor based on carbonaceous based nanomaterials was fabricated for alpha-fetoprotein detection. Herein, the composite of fullerene, graphene oxide and chitosan with specific surface area, good water dispersibility, and excellent biocompatibility was first modified onto the electrode surface and then it was in situ electrochemical reduced to improve the conductivity. After the capturing antibody was immobilized onto the modified electrode surface, the detection antibody together with poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots as the recognizable signal tags was self-assembled onto the electrode by typical sandwich immune reaction. By this design, the abundant functional groups, good water dispersibility and good electroconductivity of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots make the composite not only an immobilizing platform to carrier numerous detection antibodies, but also an promising promoter for the electrochemiluminescent signal of luminol. With the aforementioned amplify factor, the established electrochemiluminescent biosensor under the optimal conditions demonstrated a wider dynamic response from 1fgmL−1 to 80ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.33fgmL−1. Additionally, the experimental results exhibited the immunosensor possessed excellent stability, reproducibility and selectively. Accordingly, this immunosensing strategy for alpha-fetoprotein with the assistance of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers functionalized carbon nanodots provided a promising approach in clinical disease scanning.

ce:section.ce:para: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), a fetal serum protein produced by the liver and the yolk sac, has been recognized as an important biomarker for hepatocellular cancer [1]. The average level of AFP is about 25ngmL−1 in healthy human serum and the increased AFP concentration can be recognized as an early indication of cancer [2]. Accordingly, developing an accurate, quick and costless quantification strategy for AFP detection is very exigent in clinical research. Until now, a series of techniques have been exploited for the determinate of AFP, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [3], fluorescent immunoassay [4], chemiluminescent immunoassay [5], electrochemiluminescent (ECL) immunoassay [6], electrochemical immunoassay [7], and surface plasmon resonance [8].

Compared with the above mentioned methods, ECL analysis is an attractive way to detect biomarkers owing to its important application in clinical diagnosis with acknowledged advantages including high sensitivity, good sensitivity, good controllability, simplicity, rapidity, and low background signal [9–11]. Among the ECL systems, luminol is one of the most extensively implicated luminescence reagents for its excellent intrinsic properties such as nontoxicity, inexpensive, and high light-emitting quantum yield [11–13], and luminol-based ECL researches have drawn great interest in biomolecule analysis. To achieve a sensitive determination for biomarkers, many efforts have been devoting to improve the ECL signal of luminol.

Recently, many carbonaceous based nanomaterials with excellent electro-catalysis activity have been widely employed as good matrices and biological labels to enhance the ECL signal in immunosensing field [14–16]. In these carbonaceous based nanomaterials, fullerene (C60) is a truncated icosahedron with rich conjugated π electron structure made out of five and six member rings of sp2 carbons [17]. The highly symmetrical structure, unique physical and chemical properties, large surface area, superconductivity of C60 make it play an important role in the fabrication of sensitive sensing platform [18,19]. In addition, graphene oxide (GO) is a well-known two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial and has been used in different fields due to its unique physical and chemical properties [20]. The numerous oxygen functional groups on GO make hydrophobic carbon nanomaterials easily disperse into the GO aqueous solution via strong π-π interaction [21], which facilitated the further application of C60 in this biosensor. Besides, the positively charged chitosan (CS) attached onto negative charge carbon materials, providing amine groups for further covalent coupling antibody [22]. Furthermore, due to the high permeability towards water, excellent membrane-forming ability, good adhesion and susceptibility to chemical modifications of CS, the introduction of CS into the carbon materials can be used to improve the adhesion and membrane-forming ability of carbon materials to glassy carbon electrode surface, playing a key role to the subsequent fabricate process of this immunosensor [23–25]. Inspired by the advantages of C60, GO and CS, a biocompatible composite of GO@C60 was prepared and then the composite was electrochemical reduced to in situ generate reduced graphene oxide (rGO)@C60 on electrode surface to further improve the conductivity of supporting matrix because of good conductivity of C60 and rGO.

Besides, carbon nanodots (CNDs) with excellent properties including good conductivity, low cost, excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility was introduced into this system to enhance the sensitivity of this biosensor [26,27]. More importantly, the amino groups and carboxylic groups on CNDs make it forming well-dispersed aqueous colloids when dispersed in water, which make more dissolved oxygen (co-reactant) accumulate onto the surface of CNDs and significantly enhance the ECL signal of luminol [28,29]. To further improve the sensitivity of this sensor, poly(amidoamine) dendrimers (PAADs) with hundreds of amino groups in their molecular backbones was used as carriers to immobilize more CNDs through the covalent bond force [30].

Herein, rGO@C60 modified electrode with outstanding conductivity was used as sensing matrix to provide large surface area for covalent binding of capture antibody (Ab1). Covalent binding of detection antibodies (Ab2) on the PAADs@CNDs composites as sensitive ECL bioprobes were self-assembled onto the sensing interface for AFP detection by a simple sandwich immunoassay strategy. And the ECL intensity of this immunosensor increase with the increasing of target antigen concentration because of the good electrochemical and ECL catalysis toward luminol [31]. Moreover, the ultrasensitive ECL biosensor with excellent analytical performances was successfully applied to the determination of AFP in real serum samples.

Ethanol and dichloromethane were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. AFP, anti-AFP, and bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96–99%) were obtained from Biss Inc. (Beijing, China). L-Ascorbic acid (AA), glutaraldehyde (GLD), PAADs (G4) and luminol were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA) and used without further purification. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were obtained from Shanghai Medpep Co. Ltd. Other reagents were of analytical regent grade. The phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M, pH 8.0) as the supporting electrolyte was prepared by mixing stock solution of 0.1M NaH2PO4 and 0.1M Na2HPO4 and adjusting the pH. All solutions were prepared with deionized water by using a Water purifier (China) purification system.

The morphology and structural characterization were observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI F20 S-TWIN instrument). ECL intensity versus potential was detected by using a system made in our laboratory, consisting of a BPCL Ultra-Weak Chemiluminescence Analyzer controlled by a personal computer with BPCL program (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences) in conjunction with a CHI 760 electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China). The electrochemical analyzer was used for controlling waveforms and potentials. A conventional three-electrode system was used for the electrolytic system, including a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) or modified GCE as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl electrode (sat. KCl) as reference electrode. A commercial 5mL cylindroid glass cell was used as ECL cell, and it was placed directly in the front of the photomultiplier tube.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GO, C60, CNDs, GO@C60, PAADs@CNDs and PAADs@CNDs based signal tags (PAADs@CNDs@Ab2)
ce:section.ce:para: GO was prepared by typical Hummers’ methods according to our previous work [32] and C60 was synthesized according to the previous report [33]. CNDs were synthesized through one step approach according to the previous report [34]. Briefly, 2.2g AA was dissolved in 50mL of deionized water and 50mL of ethanol to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 50mL of as-prepared solution was transferred into autoclave and heated at 180°C for 4h and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Then the dark brown solution was extracted with dichloromethane. Afterwards, the water phase solution was dialyzed by employing ester dialysis membranes for three days to remove all impurity molecules. Finally, a yellow CNDs aqueous solution was obtained.

To prepare GO@C60 composite, mixing of 2mL of 2mgmL−1 C60, 2mL of 2mgmL−1 GO and 2mL of 0.3% CS solution under sonicated for 4h. Afterwards, the GO@C60 composite was obtained by centrifugation and redispersed in 2mL distilled water for the further use.

The PAADs functionalized CNDs (PAADs@CNDs) were prepared as following: 40μL of 0.2% PAADs were mixed with 50μL of GLD (5%) under vigorously stirred for 3h. Then, the covalent immobilization of CNDs onto PAADs surface can be achieved by virtue of the cross-linking reaction between aldehyde groups and amino groups. Finally, the PAADs@CNDs were obtained by centrifugation and redispersed in 2mL distilled water for the further use.

The biofunctionalized signal tags (PAADs@CNDs@Ab2) were prepared by first added 50μL of 10mgmL−1 EDC and 50μL of 20mgmL−1 NHS into 1mL of PAADs@CNDs solution. After activating the carboxyl groups of CNDs for 2h, 100μL of Ab2 was added to the above solution and reaction over night at 4°C. Then the PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 was collected by centrifugation and redispersed in 1mL PBS (pH 7.4). Following that, 1.0wt% BSA solution was added to block possible remaining active sites, and then the resulting PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 detection bioprobe was washed with PBS (pH 7.4) and redispersed in PBS (pH 7.4), and stored at 4°C until use.

Prior to the modification, the glassy carbon electrode was polished with 1, 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry and rinsed thoroughly with distilled water between each polishing step. Then it was washed successively with 1:1 nitric acid, acetone and distilled water in an ultrasonic bath and dried in air. Subsequently, 4μL of the prepared GO@C60 solution was carefully cast on the surface of the well-polished GCE and dried in air to obtain GO@C60 modified electrode. The GO and CS electrode were obtained by dropping 4μL of mixed solution contained 2mgmL−1 GO and 0.3% CS solution and 4μL of 0.3% CS solution, respectively, onto the bare GCE. Afterwards, the prepared GO@C60 and GO electrodes were electrochemically reduced at −1.3V for 400s in 0.1M KCl solution to be transferred to rGO@C60 and rGO, respectively. After that, rGO@C60 was soaked in 100μL of 100mgmL−1 Ab1 solution for 1h at 4°C. Ultimately, the excess biomolecules were removed by distilled water rinse, and the resultant electrode (rGO@C60/Ab1) was incubated in BSA solution (1%) for 30min.

The process of detection of AFP was carried out as following. The rGO@C60/Ab1 was firstly incubated in target AFP solution of different concentration for 90min to obtain rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag. Then the result electrode was submerged into 100μL of PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 solution for 80min at 4°C. After rinsing with pH 7.4 PBS to remove the unbound, the ECL response of the immunosensor was recorded in pH 8.0 PBS buffer containing 2×10−5 molL−1 luminol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the prepared GO and C60
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM of GO and C60 were performed to examine their morphologies and microstructures. As depicted in the inset of Scheme 1 , a thin and irregular flake-like two-dimensional structure can be seen from the TEM image of the prepared GO. Furthermore, some of GO sheets demonstrated a crumpled microstructure, might be owing to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the GO sheets, which the phenomenon was in agreement with the previous reports in the literature [35]. In addition, the TEM image of C60 showed the sphere-like nanoparticles of about 60nm, which was in accordance with the previous reports [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of various modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetric curves (CVs) of GO (a), GCE (b), rGO (c) and rGO@C60 (d) were explored in Fig. 1 A by using Fe(CN)6 3−/4− as redox probe. A classic pair of reversible redox peaks was obtained at bare GCE (curve b). Obviously, a significant declined peak current was depicted in curve a after GO introduced onto the bare electrode, resulting from that the poor electroconductivity of GO which hindered the transfer of electrons. As expected, an enhanced current was found in curve c due to the good conductivity of electrochemical reduced GO. It is especially noteworthy that the rGO@C60 (curve d) presented the bigger peak current than that of rGO, which indicating the excellent electrochemical catalysis capacity of C60.

In consideration of the electrochemical response being highly sensitive to the physicochemical properties of the sensing surface, background CVs were used to investigate the rGO@C60 quality in Fig. 1B. The capacitance values of modified electrodes could be approximately calculated from the CVs at 0.2V according to the equation (1) [36]:
(1)
C
C V
=
i
( s v )
Where CCV , i, s, v, represents the capacitance (Fcm−2), discharge current (A), the surface area of working electrodes (cm2), and the scan rate (Vs−1), respectively. Apparently, from the curves in Fig. 1B, rGO@C60 modified electrode owned the biggest current, implying that rGO@C60 owned highest capacitances, which may be resulted from the rich conjugated π electron structure of rGO and C60.

To evaluate the effective electroactive areas of different modified electrodes, the chronocoulometry and the linear relationship between charges and square roots of scan times were demonstrated in Fig. 1C. The effective electroactive area could be computed according to the equation (2) [37]:
(2)
Q ( t ) = k
t
1 / 2
+
Q
a d s
( k = 2 n F A c
D / π
)
Where k, t, Qads , is the slope in the linear curve, the time, and Faradic charge respectively. n represents the number of transferred electrons, F represents the Faraday constant, A represents effective electroactive area, c represents the concentration of the oxidized species, D represents the diffusion coefficient. From Fig. 1C, a maximal slope was clearly observed in curve d, suggesting that the rGO@C60 modified electrode possessed the largest effective surface area. The electroactive area of rGO@C60 was 2.34 times that of GCE and 1.1 times that of rGO, manifesting that the rGO@C60 sensing matrix could provide much more sites for antibody immobilization and electron transfer.

Moreover, the influence of scan rate on oxidation peak currents at different modified electrodes was examined by CV. As depicted in Fig. 1D, the oxidation peak currents linearly increased with the scan rate over the range of 30–120mVs−1, implying this electrochemical reaction process on the sensing interface is diffusion control process. Furthermore, according to the equation (3) [38]:
(3)
I p = k v
( k =
n
2
F
2
A Γ
/
4 R T
)
n, F, R, T were constant. A is the electrode area (cm2), Γ is the surface coverage (molcm−2). Obviously, the slope of rGO@C60 was the maximal, which was 1.38 times that of rGO and 2.69 times that of bare GCE, indicating that the rGO@C60 owned the highest electrode surface coverage.

The aforementioned findings not only convincingly demonstrated the successful synthesis of the rGO@C60 hybrid but also strongly supported the excellent electro-catalysis properties. The mainly reason is the large surface area, rich conjugated π electron structure, superior electronic conductivity of nanocarbon materials, and the good membrane-forming ability, good adhesion of CS. Besides, the NH2 groups in CS also provided a facility for the subsequent antibody immobilization. Accordingly, the synergistic effect of C60, rGO and CS make the hybrid be a promising sensing matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The tentative mechanism for the AFP detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As a proof-of-concept application, a sandwich-type immunosensor for AFP detection was established based on rGO@C60 hybrid platform in Scheme 1. In this work, Ab1 was anchored onto the rGO@C60 sensing surface through covalent coupling by virtue of the GLD as a bridging linker. Based on the typical sandwich immunosensing strategy, Ab2 labeled PAADs@CNDs as signal-on probe was connected onto the electrode surface. With the increasing of target agent concentration, more and more signal-on probe anchored onto the sensing platform, resulting in the enhancement of luminol ECL signal, thus realizing the selective detection of AFP. The possible ECL mechanism was elaborated as follows.
(4)
H
2
L −
e −
→
L
−
(5)
L
−
+
O
2
→
O
2
−
+ L
(6)
L
−
+
O
2
−
→
[
L
O
2
2 −
]
*
+
N
2
(7)
[
L
O
2
2 −
]
*
→ A
P
2 −
+ h v
When the electrode was scanned from 0V to 1.2V, luminol (H 2 L) was oxidized to an intermediate
L
−
(4). Afterwards,
O 2
was electrochemical reduced to free radical
O
2
−
(5). And then strong oxidant
O
2
−
further reacted with
L
−
to generate the excited state
[
L
O
2
2 −
]
*
(6). Finally,
[
L
O
2
2 −
]
*
fell from the excited state to the ground state, aminophthalic acid (
A
P
2 −
) and light was detected (7). Therefore, the good water dispersibility and excellent electrical conductivity of CNDs make the surface of PAADs@CNDs gather more O2 and expedite the transfer of electrons, which enhanced the ECL signal of luminol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL immunosensor characterization and detection strategy feasibility assay
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the above inferences, the immunosensor was firstly fabricated and the variation of ECL intensity during sensor fabrication was recorded. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the GCE coated by rGO@C60 (curve b) exhibited obviously enhanced ECL signal compared with the bare GCE (curve a), which proved the successful decoration and good electroconductivity of rGO@C60. However, when Ab1 (curve c) and Ag (curve d) were immobilized onto the electrode, the ECL intensity of luminol decreased successively, resulting from that the antigen-antibody complex acted as an inert electron and mass-transfer blocking layer. As anticipated, after incubating in PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 solution, a dramatically improved ECL intensity was obtained (curve e), which due to the excellent ECL catalysis toward luminol. The observations convincing demonstrated the successful fabrication of this immunosensor, which also certified the feasibility of this immunosensor for the AFP detection.

To explore the influence of carbon nanomaterials to electrochemiluminescence of luminol, the ECL intensities of different modified electrodes were examined in Fig. 2B. Compared with GCE (curve a), rGO (curve b) displayed enhanced ECL signal due to the large surface area and good ECL catalysis toward luminol. Obviously, rGO@C60 (curve c) showed greater ECL signal than that of rGO, owing to the better ECL performance resulted from synergistic effect of rGO and C60, which the phenomenon was also vividly elaborated in Fig. 2C. Besides, as depicted in Fig. 2B, after bare Ab2 was immobilized onto the sensing surface (curve d) through the immune recognition reaction, the modified electrode exhibited a very small ECL signal because the immune complex hindered the transfer of electrons. Owe to the poor conductivity of PAADs, after PAADs@Ab2 was anchored onto the electrode (curve e), there was a negligible signal was observed. When CNDs@Ab2 attached onto the electrode surface (curve f), the modified electrode depicted increased ECL intensity than that of bare Ab2, owing to the good water dispersibility and excellent electrical conductivity of CNDs make the sensing surface gather more O2 and promote the transfer of electrons. As predicted, the rGO@C60/Ab1/Ag/PAADs@CNDs@Ab2 (cureg) demonstrated the biggest ECL signal. This phenomenon can be rational explained as the fact that PAADs could carry more CNDs which produced higher ECL intensity, which the variation of ECL signal was also vividly exhibited in Fig. 2D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the optimal sensing performance, different experimental parameters were investigated in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 . As depicted in Fig. 3A and 3B, the current firstly increased and then decreased with the increasing concentration of GO and C60. It can be explained that the appropriate amount of carbon material can accelerate the electrons transfer. However, the thicker carbon film could block the electronic migration. Thus, 2mgmL−1 GO and 2mgmL−1C60 was selected for the following experiment.

Besides, the incubation time of Ab1 and Ag were important influencing factors for the sensitivity of the ECL immunosensor. As depicted in Fig. 3C, the ECL intensity at rGO@C60/Ab1 decreased rapidly from 0min to 60min and then tended to level off with further prolonging the incubation time, resulting from the approximate saturated Ab1 attached onto the surface. The similar phenomenon was also observed in Fig. 3D, which due to the recognition between Ab1 and Ag reached saturated at 80min and no further recognition with extending incubation time. Therefore, the incubation time of Ab1 and Ag was chosen 60min and 80min, respectively.

In addition, the volume ratio of CNDs and PAADs in the preparation process of signal-on probe also played a crucial role in the generation of the ECL intensity, thus the ECL intensity of different volume ratio was recorded in Fig. 4A. The ECL intensity increased rapidly as the CNDs:PAADs ratio increased from 100:1 to 500:1 and then decreased as it was increased from 500:1 to 700:1. The phenomenon could be explained as the inhibition effects of PAADs and promotion effects of CNDs to the ECL signal of luminol, leading to the intensity firstly increased with the ratio increase. However, the aggregation of more CNDs inhibited the ECL response. Accordingly, 500:1 volume ratio of CNDs and PAADs was selected for the preparation of signal-on probe.

Furthermore, the volume of Ab2 in 1mL PAADs@CNDs solution was another important factor on ECL response signal. As shown in Fig. 4B, the strongest ECL response was obtained at the volume of 100μL. Considering the detection sensitivity, 100μL of Ab2 was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Finally, the incubation time of signal-on bioprobe was also investigated in Fig. 4C. Obviously, the biggest ECL intensity was obtained at 80min, thus, 80min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

Under the optimal conditions, the typical ECL intensity-potential profile of signal-on immunosensor to the target was recorded at different AFP concentrations (Fig. 5 A). Furthermore, the ECL intensity linear increased with an increasing logarithm of AFP concentration in the range from 1 fg mL−1 to 80ngmL−1with a detection limit of 0.35 fg mL−1 (Fig. 5B), which precede the results in other reports (Table S1). The regression equation was I =2874.4+178.6 log CAFP (gmL−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.989.

To explore the specificity of the proposed immunosensor for AFP detection, the fabricated immunosensor was incubated with interferences, the coexistence of AFP and different interferent and their mixture. The corresponding results in Fig. 5C demonstrated that there were no obvious influence on the ECL intensity compared with that of AFP although the existence of a large excess of interferences, implying the high specificity of the sandwich immunosensor.

In addition, the reproducibility of the ECL biosensor was evaluated by analyzing the intra-assay and inter-assay relative standard deviation (RSD) of three different electrodes fabricated under the same condition. The intra-assay RSD of the three sensors were 3.9%, 3.1% and 3.7% at 0.01ngmL−1, 1ngmL−1 and 160ngmL−1 of AFP, respectively. Similarly, the RSD on three immunosensors were 5.6%, 5.9%, and 4.9% at 0.01ngmL−1, 1ngmL−1 and 160ngmL−1 of AFP, respectively. Therefore, the developed immunosensor possessed the good reproducibility and satisfactory precision.

Moreover, the stability of the immunosensor was examined under consecutive cyclic potential scans for 11 cycles in Fig. 5D. The result exhibited strong and stable ECL intensity without obvious descend, revealing the excellent chemical and structural stability. After the fabricated immunosensor stored at 4°C for two weeks, there was no obvious change of ECL signal, indicating the good storage stability of this sensor.

To assess the applicability of the proposed immunosensor for AFP detection in real samples, five healthy human serum samples were measured by using the fabricated ECL sensor. As depicted in Table S2, the result indicated the concentrations in the samples were lower than 10.2ngmL−1, which is very accordance with the general level of AFP in healthy humans. Besides, the recovery was performed by adding the different concentration of AFP standard solution into the serum sample in Table S2. The satisfactory results indicated that this ECL immunosensor was available for detecting AFP in real serum samples.

In summary, the promising applications of CNDs for enhancing the ECL response of luminol was explored by establishing an ultrasensitive ECL immunosensor on rGO@C60 support scaffold. Herein, the rGO@C60 composites not only provided large surface area for the subsequent immobilization of Ab1 but also furnished outstanding conductivity for the acceleration of electron transfer. In the case of CNDs, it employed as an efficient nanocarrier for immobilizing Ab2 and also as a superior conducting wire for improving electron transport. More importantly, the good water dispersibility and large specific surface of CNDs make the sensing surface of this sensor gather more co-reaction reagent, oxygen molecules, which significantly enhanced the ECL signal of luminol. By virtue of a typical immunosensing strategy, the ECL signal increasing with the increase of AFP concentration, realizing the ultrasensitive determination of AFP. Furthermore, the results demonstrated the proposed ECL biosensor owned good specificity, excellent reproducibility, outstanding stability and low cost. With these promising performances, we expect the fabricated immunosensor could be expanded for the monitoring of more tumor markers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21205016, 21575024), Program for New Century Excellent Talents of Colleges and Universities in Fujian Province, Education Department of Fujian Province (JA14071, JB13008, JA13068) and the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Fujian Province

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.02.162.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
